Definition — e-mandate (NACH / UPI AutoPay)

A one-time bank authorisation, set up via NACH or UPI AutoPay, permitting auto-debit up to a stated ceiling. The ceiling can be higher than your SIP (e.g. a ₹25,000 ceiling for a ₹5,000 SIP) so you can step up later without re-registering.

docs.title: Worked example 2 — a clean SIP setup
docs.text: You register a ₹5,000 monthly SIP on the 5th, with an e-mandate ceiling of ₹25,000. The first instalment may take a few business days for the mandate to activate; thereafter, every month on (or around) the 5th, ₹5,000 is pulled automatically, units are allotted at that day's applicable NAV, and the RTA updates your folio — no action from you. This automation, sustained for years, is the engine of rupee-cost averaging (M1) and the four lifecycle decisions you'll meet in M6.

docs.title: 6. Stage 5 — Statements and tracking
docs.text:
Once invested, the RTA keeps you informed (recall the "statements & folios" flow in M2):

Definition — CAS (Consolidated Account Statement)

A single statement, generated by the depositories/RTAs, showing all your mutual-fund (and demat) holdings across AMCs in one place. A transaction CAS is issued for the month in which you transact; a half-yearly CAS summarises holdings even if you didn't. MF Central (the joint CAMS-KFintech investor portal) lets you view and manage holdings across all fund houses in one login.

What to actually monitor (the light version; the analyst version is M9–M13): that your SIPs are going through, your units and value are updating, your contact details and nominee are current, and your KYC stays Validated.

docs.title: 7. Stage 6 — When something goes wrong: grievance & tracing
docs.text:
Two safety nets close the journey:

  • Grievance — escalate AMC → SCORES → ODR, in that order (detailed in M2 §6).
  • MITRA (Mutual Fund Investment Tracing and Retrieval Assistant) — a SEBI service (Feb 2025), hosted jointly by CAMS and KFintech and reachable via MF Central, AMFI, the QRTAs and SEBI, that helps you trace inactive or unclaimed folios — old investments forgotten, lost to outdated KYC, or left by a deceased relative.

docs.title: 8. The whole journey in one picture
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram
    participant You as Investor
    participant KRA as KYC / KRA
    participant AMC as AMC / Platform
    participant RTA as RTA (CAMS/KFintech)
    You->>KRA: Complete CKYC (PAN + Aadhaar) → aim for "Validated"
    KRA-->>You: KYC status confirmed
    You->>AMC: Open folio · choose Direct/Regular · add nominee(s)
    You->>AMC: First purchase / register SIP e-mandate
    Note over AMC: Applicable NAV by cut-off & fund realisation
    AMC->>RTA: Allot units; record folio
    RTA-->>You: Account statement / CAS
    loop Every month
        AMC->>You: Auto-debit SIP → units allotted → folio updated
    end
    Note over You,RTA: Lost a folio? → trace via MITRA

docs.title: 9. Common mistakes & Do's and Don'ts
docs.text:
Onboarding traps

  1. PAN not linked to Aadhaar → KYC On-Hold → first investment blocked. Fix linking before investing.
  2. Skipping the nominee → risk of an unclaimed, hard-to-recover folio for your heirs.
  3. Assuming the order time = your NAV → it's the realisation time vs cut-off that decides the day's NAV.
  4. Scattering money across many folios/AMCs → harder to track; use MF Central / CAS to consolidate the view.
  5. Letting KYC drift to "Registered" → a new AMC may force re-validation mid-investment.

Do

  • Do get KYC to Validated and PAN–Aadhaar linked first.
  • Do add nominees (or consciously opt out) at folio creation.
  • Do set an e-mandate ceiling above your SIP so you can step up later.
  • Do use CAS / MF Central for a single consolidated view.

Don't

  • Don't rush a lump sum near cut-off without ensuring funds realise in time.
  • Don't ignore a "Registered" or "On-Hold" KYC status.

docs.title: 10. Applicable SEBI (Mutual Funds) Regulations, 2026
docs.text:
• KYC / Aadhaar-PAN authentication — mandatory validation regime (Validated/Registered/On-Hold) effective 1 Apr 2026; PAN–Aadhaar linkage required. [verify circular ref & section no.]
  • Nomination framework — up to 10 nominees, personal nomination only, opt-out by declaration, incapacitation provisions (effective 1 Mar 2025). [verify]
  • Cut-off timing & applicable NAV — units allotted at NAV determined by fund realisation before cut-off, for all purchases (ex-liquid/overnight specifics). [verify]
  • Investor statements (CAS) — periodic consolidated statements to unit-holders. [verify]
  • MITRA — SEBI service platform (Feb 2025) to trace inactive/unclaimed folios via the QRTAs. [verify circular ref]

(KYC and grievance infrastructure as part of the wider 2026 investor-protection push is revisited in M18 and M19.)

docs.title: 11. Key takeaways
docs.text:
Key takeaways

  • The journey is friction up front, automation after — clearing KYC and folio setup is the hard part.
  • Get KYC to Validated with PAN–Aadhaar linked; "On-Hold" blocks you.
  • You can name up to 10 nominees, personally — do it (or opt out consciously).
  • The day's NAV depends on fund realisation vs cut-off, not your order time.
  • Track everything via CAS / MF Central; recover lost folios via MITRA; escalate grievances AMC → SCORES → ODR.

docs.title: 12. A word from the field
docs.text:
On letting the setup work

"The first rule of compounding: never interrupt it unnecessarily."

— Charlie Munger, Vice-Chairman of Berkshire Hathaway. The whole point of getting the journey set up cleanly — KYC, folio, nominee, an automatic SIP — is so that compounding can run uninterrupted for decades. The setup is friction; the reward is never having to touch it again.

docs.title: M6 · The Four Lifecycle Decisions
docs.text:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Frame the only four decisions an investor ever really makes — enter, stay, switch/shift, exit — and apply a rule to each.
  • Choose between lump sum, SIP and STP at entry, and explain why time in the market usually beats timing the market.
  • Decide how long to stay by horizon and goal, and recognise the behaviour (stopping SIPs in a crash) that destroys the most wealth.
  • Know the valid reasons to switch a fund and the valid triggers to exit — and the friction (exit load, tax) that should make you pause first.

This is the beginner version. Each decision here returns in quantitative form later: entry in M12, monitoring/rebalancing in M13, switch/exit tax-math in M14. Tax mechanics themselves are M8 — here we keep tax as a "friction to remember", not a calculation.

docs.title: 1. Intuition first — investing is just four decisions, repeated
docs.text:
Strip away the noise and a fund investor faces exactly four decisions across a lifetime:

flowchart LR
    E["1 · ENTER<br/>how to put money in"] --> S["2 · STAY<br/>how long to hold"]
    S --> W["3 · SWITCH / SHIFT<br/>rebalance or change fund"]
    W --> X["4 · EXIT<br/>when to take money out"]
    W -.-> S
    classDef d fill:#e8eaf6,stroke:#3949ab,color:#1a237e;
    class E,S,W,X d;

Most investing mistakes are not stock-picking errors — they are decision errors at these four points: entering all at once and panicking, staying too short, switching for the wrong reason, or exiting in fear. Get the four right and average funds will serve you well; get them wrong and the best fund won't save you.

docs.title: 2. Decision 1 — When and how to enter
docs.text:
The question is rarely "should I invest?" but "how should I deploy the money I have?" Three tools:

  • SIP (Systematic Investment Plan) — fixed amount monthly. Best for money you earn over time (your salary). It removes timing from the decision and gives rupee-cost averaging (M1).
  • Lump sum — invest a large amount at once. Best when you have a windfall and a long horizon; statistically it often beats staggering because markets rise more often than they fall — but it carries timing regret if a crash follows.
  • STP (Systematic Transfer Plan) — park a lump sum in a liquid/debt fund and transfer a fixed amount into an equity fund each month. A compromise: the un-deployed money earns a liquid return while you average in.

Definition — STP (Systematic Transfer Plan)

A facility to move a fixed sum periodically from one scheme (usually liquid/debt) to another (usually equity) within the same AMC. It is the lump-sum investor's version of an SIP — averaging entry while parking the rest productively.

docs.title: Worked example 1 — lump sum vs STP for ₹12,00,000
docs.text:
You receive a ₹12,00,000 bonus and want it in equity.

  • Lump sum: all ₹12L in equity today. If the market rises steadily, you capture the full rise — usually the higher-return path. If it falls 20% next month, you feel every rupee of it.
  • STP: park ₹12L in a liquid fund (earning ~6–7%), transfer ₹1,00,000/month to equity over 12 months. You average your entry price, the parked balance keeps earning, and a near-term crash hurts far less. The cost: in a rising market you give up some upside on the money still waiting.

The rule: for a long horizon and steady nerves, lump sum is statistically fine; if a near-term fall would make you panic-sell, STP buys you the discipline to stay invested — and discipline is worth more than the small expected-return give-up.

Why not just wait for a dip?

Because the dip you wait for may come only after a 30% rise you missed. Time in the market compounds; timing the market requires being right twice (when to leave, when to return) and most people are right neither time.

docs.title: 3. Decision 2 — How long to stay
docs.text:
The holding period should be set by the goal's horizon, decided before you invest — not by how the fund is doing.

Horizon to the goal Sensible vehicle Why < 1 year Liquid / overnight No time to recover from equity falls 1–3 years Short-duration debt / arbitrage Low volatility, modest growth 3–5 years Hybrid / balanced advantage Cushioned equity exposure 5+ years Equity (diversified) Time for volatility to wash out

The single most important behaviour is not interrupting an SIP during a fall. A market crash is when your fixed SIP buys the most units at the lowest NAVs — exactly the fuel for the eventual recovery. Stopping the SIP in fear converts a temporary dip into a permanent loss of those cheap units.

docs.title: Worked example 2 — the cost of pausing in a crash
docs.text: You run a ₹10,000 SIP. In a sharp fall the NAV drops from ₹100 to ₹70. Your ₹10,000 now buys 142.9 units instead of 100. If you pause the SIP "until things stabilise" and the NAV recovers to ₹110, you bought zero cheap units and resume only after the recovery — the most expensive possible behaviour. Staying invested means those low-NAV months become your best-performing instalments.

docs.title: 4. Decision 3 — When and how to switch or shift
docs.text:
"Switch/shift" covers two very different actions:

(a) Rebalancing (shifting between asset classes). Over time a rally pushes your equity weight above target; you shift some back to debt to restore your chosen risk. This is mechanical and good — it sells high and buys low automatically.

(b) Changing a fund (switching schemes). Replacing one fund with another. This is justified by valid reasons only:

  • The fund drifts from its mandate / breaches true-to-label (M3).
  • A persistent underperformance versus its category over a meaningful period (not one bad quarter).
  • A fundamental change — manager/AMC change you don't trust, mandate change, strategy change.
  • Your own asset allocation or goal changed.

Switching has friction — pause before you do it

A switch is a redeem + re-buy: it can trigger an exit load (if within the load period) and a capital-gains tax event (computed in M8/M14). Chasing last year's top performer by switching often costs more in tax and friction than the performance gap you're chasing. Rebalance for risk; switch only for cause.

docs.title: Worked example 3 — a rebalance in numbers
docs.text: Your target is 60% equity / 40% debt on a ₹10,00,000 portfolio (₹6L/₹4L). A strong year takes equity to ₹8L and debt to ₹4.2L — total ₹12.2L, now 65.6% / 34.4%. To restore 60/40 you need ₹7.32L equity / ₹4.88L debt, so you shift ₹0.68L from equity to debt (ideally via an STP, and mindful of tax). You have just sold equity high and bought debt low — without forecasting anything.

docs.title: 5. Decision 4 — When to exit
docs.text:
Exiting should be planned, driven by one of three triggers — never by fear:

  1. The goal is near. As a goal comes within ~2–3 years, glide money from equity to safer assets so a late crash can't derail it. (Life Cycle Funds from M3 automate exactly this glide path.)
  2. Asset allocation can't be restored by rebalancing, or your risk capacity changed.
  3. A fundamental, lasting problem with the fund that switching to a peer won't fix.

For income rather than a full exit, use an SWP:

Definition — SWP (Systematic Withdrawal Plan)

A facility to withdraw a fixed amount periodically from a fund — the mirror image of an SIP. Used in retirement to draw a monthly "paycheck" from a corpus while the remainder stays invested. (Its tax efficiency vs IDCW is shown in M8.)

docs.title: Worked example 4 — gliding out as a goal nears
docs.text: Your child's college fee of ₹15,00,000 is due in 3 years and you're at ₹14L in equity. A 30% crash in year 2 would leave ₹9.8L — a disaster with no time to recover. So you glide: move (say) one-third to debt now, another third next year, so that by the goal year most of the corpus is in short-duration debt and certain. You trade a little expected upside for the certainty of meeting a non-negotiable goal — the right trade when the money is needed, not merely wanted.

docs.title: 6. Common mistakes & Do's and Don'ts
docs.text:
Behavioural traps (the real enemy)

  1. Stopping an SIP in a crash — forfeits the cheapest units; the costliest mistake of all.
  2. Performance-chasing switches — buying last year's winner, paying tax and load, then watching it mean-revert.
  3. Waiting for the "right time" to enter — the cost of being out usually exceeds the cost of a bad entry.
  4. Exiting in panic — converting a paper dip into a realised loss.
  5. Setting the horizon by the fund's mood, not by the goal decided up front.

Do

  • Do decide the horizon and exit plan before you invest.
  • Do use SIP for income-flow money, STP to deploy a lump sum you'd panic over.
  • Do rebalance for risk on a schedule; switch only for cause.
  • Do glide to safety as a needed goal approaches.

Don't

  • Don't interrupt SIPs in downturns.
  • Don't switch funds for last year's returns without counting load + tax.

docs.title: 7. Applicable SEBI (Mutual Funds) Regulations, 2026
docs.text:
• Exit load framework — schemes may levy an exit load to deter quick redemptions; relevant to every switch/exit decision (and recall the 2026 removal of the 5 bps exit-load TER allowance, M4). [verify section no.]
  • True-to-label & categorisation — the legal basis for a valid "mandate drift" switch trigger (M3). [verify]
  • Riskometer & product labelling — schemes must display a standard risk label, helping the horizon-matching in Decision 2. [verify]
  • Life Cycle Funds — the 2026 glide-path category that automates the Decision-4 exit glide. [verify]
  • SIP/STP/SWP are scheme facilities operated within these rules, not separate regulations.

(The quantitative versions — valuation-aware entry, rebalancing bands, tax-aware exit calculus — are M12–M14.)

docs.title: 8. Key takeaways
docs.text:
Key takeaways

  • Investing is four decisions: enter, stay, switch/shift, exit — most losses are decision errors here, not fund-selection errors.
  • Enter by SIP (for salary) or STP (for a lump sum you'd panic over); don't wait for a perfect dip.
  • Stay for the goal's horizon, and never stop an SIP in a crash — that's when it buys the cheapest units.
  • Rebalance for risk; switch funds only for cause — and count exit load + tax first.
  • Exit on a plan: glide to safety as the goal nears; use SWP for income, not panic.

docs.title: 9. A word from the field
docs.text:
On timing the market

"Far more money has been lost by investors preparing for corrections, or trying to anticipate corrections, than has been lost in the corrections themselves."

— Peter Lynch, One Up on Wall Street. The four decisions are won by discipline and patience, not prediction: stay invested, automate, rebalance mechanically, and exit on a plan — not on a forecast.

docs.title: M7 · Reading a Factsheet & SID Like a Professional
docs.text:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Tell apart the four documents a fund publishes — SID, KIM, SAI, and the monthly factsheet — and know what each is for.
  • Read every line of a factsheet: benchmark, riskometer, AUM, manager tenure, expense ratio, holdings, concentration, allocation, and the ratios.
  • Interpret debt-specific disclosures — YTM, duration, the PRC matrix, credit profile — at a beginner level.
  • Read returns correctly (point-to-point vs rolling, vs the right TRI benchmark) and spot the red flags.

Builds on M3 (categories), M4 (cost) and M6 (decisions). This is where you learn to judge a fund from its documents rather than its advertising. The heavy ratio math (Sharpe, alpha, duration analytics) is only introduced here and fully built in M9–M11.

docs.title: 1. Intuition first — read the report card, not the poster
docs.text: Every fund publishes, by law, a set of documents. An advertisement shows you a fund's best face; the factsheet and the Scheme Information Document (SID) show you what it actually holds, what it costs, what it benchmarks against, and how risky it is. Learning to read them is the difference between being sold a fund and choosing one. The good news: factsheets are standardised, so once you can read one, you can read all of them.

docs.title: 2. The four documents and what each is for
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    SAI["SAI — Statement of Additional Information<br/>the AMC-level legal/statutory detail"]
    SID["SID — Scheme Information Document<br/>the scheme's full 'contract': objective, strategy,<br/>risks, fees, rules"]
    KIM["KIM — Key Information Memorandum<br/>the short-form summary of the SID (application form)"]
    FS["Factsheet (monthly)<br/>the scheme's monthly 'report card':<br/>holdings, returns, ratios"]
    SID --> KIM
    SAI -.->|referenced by| SID
    SID -.->|updated monthly by| FS

    classDef legal fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#0d47a1;
    classDef live fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20;
    class SAI,SID,KIM legal;
    class FS live;
  • SID (Scheme Information Document) — the scheme's full legal document: investment objective, strategy, asset allocation ranges, risk factors, fees, load structure, and rules. Your binding "contract" with the scheme. The 2026 reforms simplified and shortened these documents.
  • KIM (Key Information Memorandum) — the SID's short-form summary, attached to the application form.
  • SAI (Statement of Additional Information) — AMC-level statutory information (the trustees, auditors, procedures), referenced by every SID.
  • Factsheet — the monthly report card: current holdings, allocation, returns and ratios. This is what you read regularly; the SID is what you read once, before buying.

Which to read when

Before buying: read the SID (objective, risk, load, mandate). While holding: read the factsheet monthly (holdings, returns, ratios, any drift).

docs.title: 3. Anatomy of a factsheet — an annotated walkthrough
docs.text:
Below is an illustrative (not real) factsheet for a fictional "Illustrative Flexi Cap Fund — Direct, Growth", annotated line by line.

Factsheet line Illustrative value What it tells you Category Flexi Cap The mandate rules from M3 (≥65% equity, any cap mix). Investment objective Long-term growth, flexible market-cap Must match your goal/horizon. Benchmark (Tier-1) Nifty 500 TRI The yardstick. Must be a Total Return Index (see §5). Riskometer Very High SEBI's 6-level risk label (Low → Very High). Inception date Jan 2013 Length of live track record (~13 yrs). AUM ₹18,450 cr Fund size; affects agility and cost slab (M4). Fund manager (tenure) "A. Sharma" — since 2016 (~10 yrs) Who runs it and for how long; a recent manager change weakens the track record's relevance. Expense ratio Direct 0.78% / Regular 1.62% Cost — read post-2026 as BER + add-ons (M4). No. of stocks 58 Diversification breadth. Top-10 concentration 41% of portfolio How concentrated the bets are. Market-cap split Large 68% / Mid 21% / Small 11% The fund's real risk posture (a flexi-cap's choice). Sector allocation Financials 28%, IT 14%, … Where the active bets are. Portfolio turnover 38% How much trading the manager does (see §4). Std dev / Beta / Sharpe 13.2% / 0.92 / 0.71 Risk & risk-adjusted return — introduced here, computed in M9.

docs.title: How to actually read it (the order that matters)
docs.text:
1. Does the category and objective match my goal and horizon? (If not, stop — nothing else matters.)
  2. Is the benchmark a TRI, and how has the fund done against it over 3/5/10 years and rolling? (Beating the fund's own past is meaningless; beating its benchmark is the test.)
  3. What is the real risk posture — riskometer, market-cap split, top-10 concentration, sector bets?
  4. What does it cost (direct TER), and who runs it, for how long?
  5. Only then, the ratios.

docs.title: Worked example 1 — concentration tells a story
docs.text: Two flexi-cap funds both returned ~16% over five years. Fund A holds 58 stocks, top-10 = 41%; Fund B holds 28 stocks, top-10 = 64%. Same return, very different risk: Fund B's result rode on a few large bets — higher potential, higher fragility. The factsheet's concentration lines reveal what the headline return hides. (Whether B's extra risk was rewarded is the Sharpe/alpha question of M9–M10.)

docs.title: 4. Debt-fund disclosures — three numbers and a matrix
docs.text:
A debt fund's factsheet replaces market-cap and sector lines with credit and duration disclosures (full analytics in M11). Read four things:

Definitions — the debt factsheet vitals

  • YTM (Yield to Maturity) — the annualised return if all holdings are held to maturity; a rough gross indicator of the income the portfolio can generate (before TER and defaults).
  • Macaulay / Modified duration — the portfolio's interest-rate sensitivity: a modified duration of 1.6 means a 1% rise in rates cuts NAV ~1.6%. Longer = more rate risk.
  • Credit profile — the split by rating (e.g. AAA 72% / AA 23% / A 5%); lower-rated = higher yield and higher default risk.
  • PRC (Potential Risk Class) matrix — a SEBI grid placing the scheme by max interest-rate risk (Class A/B/C) × max credit risk (Class I/II/III). It caps how much risk the fund is allowed to take.

docs.title: The PRC matrix at a glance
docs.text: Credit Risk I (lowest) Credit Risk II Credit Risk III (highest) Interest-rate Class A (lowest) A-I A-II A-III Interest-rate Class B B-I B-II ← example fund B-III Interest-rate Class C (highest) C-I C-II C-III

docs.title: Worked example 2 — reading a short-duration fund
docs.text: A short-duration fund shows YTM 7.6%, Modified duration 1.6, AAA 72% / AA 23% / A 5%, PRC = B-II. Reading it: it can earn ~7.6% gross if nothing defaults; a 1% rate rise would knock ~1.6% off NAV (modest); roughly a quarter of the book is below AAA (some credit risk); and the B-II label legally caps it at moderate rate-and-credit risk. A sensible 1–3 year parking choice — not a substitute for a liquid fund or an FD.

docs.title: 5. Reading returns honestly
docs.text:
Two traps live in the returns table:

  • Point-to-point vs rolling returns. A "5-year return of 19%" is one lucky window (point-to-point). Rolling returns (the average of all 5-year windows) show consistency — far more trustworthy. Prefer rolling.
  • The benchmark must be a TRI. A Total Return Index includes dividends; comparing a fund against a price-only index flatters the fund. Since SEBI's mandate, funds benchmark against TRI and often a two-tier benchmark (Tier-1 broad category index; Tier-2 a more specific index).

Definition — Total Return Index (TRI)

An index that assumes dividends are reinvested, so it reflects the full return of holding the index. Benchmarking against TRI is the honest test — it is the return you could have earned passively, dividends included.

docs.title: 6. Red flags to catch on any factsheet
docs.text:
Warning signs

  1. Recent manager change — a long track record means little if the person who built it just left.
  2. Mandate drift — a "large-cap" fund heavy in mid/small caps (a true-to-label breach, M3).
  3. Soaring AUM in a small/mid-cap fund — size can blunt agility; watch the stress-test/liquidity disclosure.
  4. High portfolio turnover with no outperformance — lots of trading (and cost) for nothing.
  5. Benchmark that isn't a TRI, or cherry-picked point-to-point windows in marketing.
  6. Rising credit risk in a debt fund (drifting from AAA toward lower ratings to chase YTM).

docs.title: 7. Common mistakes & Do's and Don'ts
docs.text:
Misreadings

  1. Judging a fund by its own past, not its benchmark. Beating itself is automatic; beating the TRI is the test.
  2. Reading point-to-point returns as typical. Use rolling returns.
  3. Ignoring the riskometer / PRC because the return looked good.
  4. Treating a high YTM as "more return" — it often signals more credit risk, not free yield.

Do

  • Do read the SID before buying, the factsheet monthly after.
  • Do compare returns to the TRI benchmark and prefer rolling returns.
  • Do check manager tenure, concentration, and (for debt) PRC + credit profile.

Don't

  • Don't chase a high YTM or a single lucky return window.
  • Don't skip the riskometer and asset-allocation lines.

docs.title: 8. Applicable SEBI (Mutual Funds) Regulations, 2026
docs.text:
• Standardised scheme documents — SID, KIM and SAI in prescribed formats; 2026 simplification/shortening of these documents. [verify section no.]
  • Benchmarking — two-tier, Total Return Index benchmarks for performance disclosure. [verify circular ref]
  • Riskometer & product labelling — mandatory 6-level riskometer; PRC matrix for debt schemes. [verify]
  • Portfolio & performance disclosure — periodic portfolio disclosure (monthly/fortnightly for debt), and standardised return reporting; stress-testing/liquidity disclosure for mid/small-cap schemes. [verify]
  • True-to-label — the basis for spotting mandate drift on a factsheet (M3). [verify]

(Full disclosure architecture and the compliance timeline are in M18.)

docs.title: 9. Key takeaways
docs.text:
Key takeaways

  • SID = the contract (read before buying); factsheet = the monthly report card (read while holding).
  • Read a factsheet in order: objective/category → return vs TRI benchmark → risk posture → cost → manager → ratios.
  • Concentration, manager tenure and market-cap split reveal risk the headline return hides.
  • For debt, read YTM, duration, credit profile and the PRC class — a high YTM usually means more credit risk.
  • Trust rolling returns vs a TRI, not point-to-point windows; watch for manager change and mandate drift.

docs.title: 10. A word from the field
docs.text:
On doing the homework

"Investment is most intelligent when it is most businesslike."

— Benjamin Graham, The Intelligent Investor (a line Warren Buffett calls the most important words ever written about investing). Reading the SID and factsheet is the businesslike act: you are inspecting the product's books before committing capital, rather than buying on a story.

docs.title: M8 · Taxation
docs.text:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Place any fund into the three tax buckets (equity-oriented / specified-debt / "other") and apply the correct rate and holding period.
  • Compute STCG and LTCG on equity funds, including the ₹1.25 lakh annual exemption, at mid-2026 rates.
  • Apply the Section 50AA slab treatment to post-April-2023 debt funds, and the 24-month / 12.5% rule to gold, international and other funds.
  • Tax IDCW, understand why an SWP is more tax-efficient, and remember that a switch is a taxable event.

Currency

All rates are current to mid-2026 (FY 2026-27 / AY 2027-28). Budget 2025 made no change to these capital-gains rules. Tax is personal — figures illustrate the mechanics; consult a tax adviser for your situation. (This is information, not tax advice.)

Builds on M3 (a fund's category determines its tax) and M6 (switch/exit decisions carry tax friction). Tax mechanics here feed the tax-aware exit calculus of M14.

docs.title: 1. Intuition first — tax follows what the fund holds, not its name
docs.text:
The single rule that organises everything: a fund is taxed by what it predominantly holds, and by how long you held it. "Equity-oriented" (mostly Indian shares) gets the friendliest treatment; "debt" gets the harshest (since 2023); everything in between sits in a third bucket. Get the bucket right first, then the holding period, then the rate.

flowchart TD
    F["Your fund"] --> Q1{"≥65% in Indian equity?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> EQ["BUCKET 1 · Equity-oriented<br/>(equity, ELSS, arbitrage,<br/>aggressive hybrid, most BAF)"]
    Q1 -- "No" --> Q2{">65% in debt /<br/>money-market?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> DEBT["BUCKET 2 · Specified debt fund<br/>(debt, liquid, conservative hybrid)"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> OTH["BUCKET 3 · 'Other'<br/>(gold, international, FoF,<br/>35–65% hybrids, multi-asset)"]

    EQ --> EQr["≤12m → STCG 20%<br/>>12m → LTCG 12.5% over ₹1.25L"]
    DEBT --> DEBTr["Bought on/after 1 Apr 2023:<br/>always at SLAB (Sec 50AA)"]
    OTH --> OTHr["≤24m → SLAB<br/>>24m → 12.5% (no ₹1.25L exemption)"]

    classDef a fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20;
    classDef b fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,color:#b71c1c;
    classDef c fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f9a825,color:#7a5b00;
    class EQ,EQr a;
    class DEBT,DEBTr b;
    class OTH,OTHr c;

docs.title: 2. Bucket 1 — Equity-oriented funds (≥65% Indian equity)
docs.text:
The friendliest bucket. Includes pure equity funds, ELSS, arbitrage funds, aggressive hybrids, and balanced-advantage/equity-savings funds that keep ≥65% equity.

Equity fund capital-gains rates (mid-2026)

  • Short-term (held ≤ 12 months) — STCG 20% (Section 111A; raised from 15% on 23 July 2024).
  • Long-term (held > 12 months) — LTCG 12.5% on gains above ₹1.25 lakh per financial year (Section 112A). The first ₹1.25 lakh of LTCG each year is exempt.
  • STT (Securities Transaction Tax) applies on redemption; a 4% health & education cess (and surcharge where applicable, LTCG surcharge capped at 15%) sits on top of the rates above.

docs.title: Worked example 1 — equity LTCG with the exemption
docs.text:
You invest ₹5,00,000, redeem after 3 years at ₹8,00,000. Gain = ₹3,00,000 (long-term).

Taxable LTCG = 3,00,000 - 1,25,000 (exemption) = 1,75,000 Tax = 1,75,000 × 12.5% = ₹21,875 (+ 4% cess ⇒ ₹22,750)

Your effective tax on a ₹3 lakh gain is ~7.6% — the exemption plus the low LTCG rate make long-held equity highly tax-efficient.

docs.title: Worked example 2 — equity STCG
docs.text:
Same fund, but you redeem after 10 months with a ₹50,000 gain (short-term):

Tax = 50,000 × 20% = ₹10,000 (+cess)

No ₹1.25 lakh exemption applies to STCG. The lesson from M6 is now visible in rupees: holding past 12 months drops the rate from 20% to 12.5% and unlocks the exemption — a powerful reason not to churn.

Tax-harvesting the ₹1.25 lakh exemption

Because the LTCG exemption is per financial year, long-term investors can redeem and immediately re-buy to "harvest" up to ₹1.25 lakh of gains tax-free each year, resetting their cost base. Useful, but mind exit loads, the re-buy NAV, and that it only helps long-term equity gains. (Detailed in M14.)

docs.title: 3. Bucket 2 — Specified debt funds (>65% debt), post-April-2023
docs.text:
The harshest bucket, after a structural 2023 change.

Section 50AA — specified mutual funds

A "specified mutual fund" invests more than 65% in debt/money-market instruments (debt funds, liquid funds, most conservative hybrids). For units acquired on or after 1 April 2023, all gains are taxed at your income-tax slab rate, regardless of holding period — there is no LTCG rate and no indexation. (Units bought before 1 April 2023 retain older treatment: >24 months → 12.5% without indexation post-July-2024.)

docs.title: Worked example 3 — a debt fund bought in 2024
docs.text:
You invest ₹2,00,000 in a debt fund in 2024, redeem after 3 years at ₹2,40,000. Gain = ₹40,000. If you are in the 30% slab:

Tax = 40,000 × 30% = ₹12,000 (+cess)

The 3-year holding earns no concession — the gain is taxed exactly as salary would be. This is the change that erased debt funds' old tax edge over fixed deposits, reshaping how HNIs and institutions allocate (a structural fact we return to in M16).

docs.title: 4. Bucket 3 — "Other" funds (gold, international, FoF, 35–65% hybrids)
docs.text:
Funds that are neither ≥65% Indian equity nor >65% debt — gold funds, international/overseas funds, fund-of-funds, multi-asset and 35–65% hybrids — sit in a middle bucket:

'Other' fund rates (mid-2026)

  • Short-term (held ≤ 24 months) — taxed at slab.
  • Long-term (held > 24 months) — 12.5% without indexation.
  • No ₹1.25 lakh exemption (that is only for equity-oriented Section 112A funds).

docs.title: Worked example 4 — a gold or international fund
docs.text:
You invest ₹3,00,000 in a gold fund, redeem after 30 months at ₹4,00,000. Gain = ₹1,00,000 (long-term, >24m):

Tax = 1,00,000 × 12.5% = ₹12,500 (+cess)

Had you sold at 18 months, the whole ₹1,00,000 would be taxed at your slab — so the 24-month line matters a lot for this bucket. Note there is no ₹1.25 lakh exemption here, unlike equity.

docs.title: 5. IDCW, SWP and the switch — three things people get wrong
5.1 IDCW is taxed at your slab
docs.text: The IDCW payout (the old "dividend", M1) is added to your income and taxed at your slab rate. The fund deducts TDS at 10% once IDCW from a single fund crosses the annual threshold (₹10,000 from FY 2025-26) [verify threshold]. Combined with the fact that IDCW is just your own capital returned (NAV falls by the payout), this makes IDCW tax-inefficient for most investors.

docs.title: 5.2 SWP is far more tax-efficient than IDCW
docs.text: An SWP (systematic withdrawal, M6) is treated as a redemption, so only the capital-gain portion of each withdrawal is taxed — not the whole amount.

docs.title: Worked example 5 — SWP vs IDCW for ₹50,000 of monthly income
docs.text:
You hold ₹10,00,000 (cost ₹8,00,000, so the corpus is 20% gain). You want ₹50,000:

  • Via IDCW: the entire ₹50,000 is income, taxed at your slab (say 30%) → ₹15,000 tax.
  • Via SWP: of the ₹50,000 withdrawn, only ~20% (₹10,000) is gain; the rest is your own capital. If it's long-term equity, that ₹10,000 may even fall within the ₹1.25 lakh exemption → ₹0–₹1,250 tax.

Same cash in hand, a fraction of the tax. For regular income, prefer an SWP over IDCW.

docs.title: 5.3 A switch is a taxable event
docs.text: Switching from Fund A to Fund B (or regular→direct, or between schemes) is a redemption of A + purchase of B — it realises capital gains on A and may trigger an exit load. This is the friction M6 warned about and M14 quantifies: never switch on a whim without counting the tax.

docs.title: 6. ELSS and the 80C question under the new regime
docs.text:
ELSS (Equity-Linked Savings Scheme, M3) offers a deduction of up to ₹1.5 lakh under Section 80C — but only under the OLD tax regime. Under the new regime (now the default), there is no 80C deduction, so ELSS loses its tax-saving rationale and becomes just an equity fund with a 3-year lock-in. Whether ELSS still makes sense depends entirely on which regime you are in.

Check your regime first

If you are on the new regime, ELSS gives you no deduction — don't buy it for tax saving. If on the old regime, ELSS is the shortest-lock-in 80C option. The regime choice comes first.

docs.title: 7. The whole tax picture in one table
docs.text:
Fund type Bucket Short-term Long-term LT threshold ₹1.25L exemption? Equity, ELSS, arbitrage, ≥65% hybrid 1 20% (≤12m) 12.5% (>12m) 12 months Yes Debt/liquid (bought ≥ Apr 2023) 2 Slab (any period) — — No Gold, international, FoF, 35–65% hybrid 3 Slab (≤24m) 12.5% (>24m) 24 months No

(All + 4% cess; surcharge where applicable. IDCW always at slab with TDS.)

docs.title: 8. Common mistakes & Do's and Don'ts
docs.text:
Tax traps

  1. Assuming all "hybrid" funds are equity-taxed. Only ≥65%-equity hybrids are; a conservative hybrid may be debt-taxed (Bucket 2).
  2. Expecting LTCG benefit on a post-2023 debt fund. There is none — it's slab, always.
  3. Treating IDCW as efficient income. It's taxed at slab and is your own capital; an SWP is better.
  4. Forgetting a switch is taxable. Chasing a marginally better fund can cost more in tax than it gains.
  5. Buying ELSS for tax saving on the new regime — no deduction there.
  6. Wasting the ₹1.25L exemption by never harvesting it.

Do

  • Do identify the bucket before estimating tax.
  • Do hold equity past 12 months to get 12.5% + the exemption.
  • Do use an SWP for income and harvest the ₹1.25L exemption yearly.

Don't

  • Don't churn equity inside 12 months without a strong reason (20% STCG).
  • Don't ignore tax when switching funds.

docs.title: 9. Applicable law & SEBI (Mutual Funds) Regulations, 2026
docs.text:
Taxation lives in the Income-tax Act (Sections 111A, 112A, 50AA), not the SEBI Regulations — but the Regulations supply the definitions the tax law relies on:

  • Equity-oriented (≥65%) classification and categorisation (M3) determine which tax bucket a fund falls into. [verify cross-reference]
  • True-to-label ensures a fund's stated equity/debt mix — and therefore its tax treatment — is real. [verify]
  • Specified mutual fund (>65% debt) under Sec 50AA keys off the same portfolio-composition rules SEBI mandates.
  • Income-tax provisions: Sec 111A (equity STCG 20%), Sec 112A (equity LTCG 12.5% over ₹1.25L), Sec 50AA (specified funds at slab), Sec 194K (IDCW TDS). [verify TDS threshold for FY 2026-27]

docs.title: 10. Key takeaways
docs.text:
Key takeaways

  • Tax follows what the fund holds → put it in Bucket 1 (equity) / 2 (specified debt) / 3 (other) first.
  • Equity: STCG 20% (≤12m); LTCG 12.5% over a ₹1.25L yearly exemption (>12m).
  • Specified debt (post-Apr-2023): always slab — no LTCG, no indexation (Sec 50AA).
  • Other (gold/international/FoF): slab ≤24m, 12.5% >24m, no exemption.
  • SWP beats IDCW on tax; a switch is a taxable event; ELSS 80C only helps on the old regime.

docs.title: 11. A word from the field
docs.text:
On certainty

"In this world nothing can be said to be certain, except death and taxes."

— Benjamin Franklin, letter to Jean-Baptiste Le Roy, 1789. Since the tax is certain, the intelligent move is not to avoid it but to arrange holdings tax-efficiently — hold equity long, prefer SWP to IDCW, mind the buckets — so that more of a given return is kept.

docs.title: M9 · Risk-Adjusted Performance
docs.text:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Explain why raw return is misleading and define CAGR, volatility (standard deviation), downside deviation and maximum drawdown.
  • Compute and interpret the four workhorse ratios — Sharpe, Sortino, Treynor, Information Ratio — on Indian figures.
  • Choose the right ratio for the question being asked, and read a risk-return scatter.
  • Avoid the classic misuses (annualisation errors, cross-period and cross-asset comparisons, too-short samples).

This opens Level 3 (Analyst). It gives the math behind the factsheet ratios introduced in M7, and the risk vocabulary the rest of L3 (M10–M14) relies on. Beta and alpha are used here and built in M10.

docs.title: 1. Intuition first — return per unit of risk
docs.text: Two funds each returned 13% over five years. One did it with a smooth ride; the other lurched through a 40% crash on the way. They are not equally good. The second investor took far more risk for the same reward — and was far more likely to panic-sell at the bottom (M6). Professional evaluation therefore never asks "what was the return?" alone; it asks "what was the return per unit of risk taken?" Every ratio in this module is a different answer to that one question.

docs.title: 2. The raw return — CAGR
docs.text:
Before adjusting for risk, measure return correctly. A multi-year result must be annualised as a compound rate, not a simple average.

Formula — CAGR (Compound Annual Growth Rate)

CAGR = (Ending value / Beginning value)1/n − 1 where n = number of years.

docs.title: Worked example 1 — CAGR
docs.text: ₹1,00,000 grows to ₹2,01,000 in 6 years: CAGR = (2.01)1/6 - 1 = 1.1234 - 1 = 12.34% This single number is comparable across funds and periods — unlike "the fund doubled", which hides how long it took.

docs.title: 3. Measuring risk — four lenses
docs.text:
Definitions — the risk measures

  • Standard deviation (σ, volatility) — how much returns scatter around their average. Computed from periodic (e.g. monthly) returns and annualised: σannual = σmonthly × √(12). Higher σ = a bumpier ride.
  • Downside deviation — like σ but counts only returns below a target (often the risk-free rate or zero). It ignores "good" volatility (upside), capturing only the risk you actually dislike.
  • Maximum drawdown — the largest peak-to-trough fall over the period. Answers "what is the worst it put me through?" — the number that predicts panic-selling.
  • Beta (β) — sensitivity to the market: β = 1.1 means the fund tends to move 1.1× the index. Introduced here; derived in M10.

Each lens matters for a different reason: σ for overall bumpiness, downside deviation for the bad bumpiness, drawdown for survivability, beta for market-linked risk.

docs.title: 4. The four risk-adjusted ratios
docs.text:
We will use one consistent dataset throughout. Risk-free rate Rf = 6.5% (roughly a short Indian G-sec/T-bill yield).

Return σ Downside dev Beta vs Benchmark (11.5%) Tracking error Fund A 14% 18% 12% 1.10 +2.5% 5% Fund B 12% 11% 7% 0.80 +0.5% 3%

docs.title: 4.1 Sharpe ratio — excess return per unit of total risk
docs.text:
Formula — Sharpe ratio

Sharpe = (Rp - Rf) / σp Excess return (over the risk-free rate) per unit of total volatility. Higher is better. The single most-used measure.

SharpeA = (14 - 6.5) / 18 = 0.42 SharpeB = (12 - 6.5) / 11 = 0.50

Fund B wins despite its lower return — it delivered more reward per unit of risk. This is the module's central lesson in one line: higher return ≠ better fund.

docs.title: 4.2 Sortino ratio — excess return per unit of downside risk
docs.text:
Formula — Sortino ratio

Sortino = (Rp - Rf) / (Downside deviation) Like Sharpe, but penalises only harmful (downside) volatility — fairer to funds whose volatility is mostly upside.

SortinoA = 7.5 / 12 = 0.63 SortinoB = 5.5 / 7 = 0.79

Fund B again leads — its losses, specifically, were better contained.

docs.title: 4.3 Treynor ratio — excess return per unit of market risk
docs.text:
Formula — Treynor ratio

Treynor = (Rp - Rf) / βp Excess return per unit of beta (market risk only). Appropriate when the fund is one part of an already-diversified portfolio, so only its market risk matters.

TreynorA = 7.5 / 1.10 = 6.82 TreynorB = 5.5 / 0.80 = 6.88

Close, with B marginally ahead.

docs.title: 4.4 Information Ratio — active skill per unit of active risk
docs.text:
Formula — Information Ratio (IR)

IR = (Rp - Rb) / (Tracking error) Return above the benchmark per unit of tracking error (how much the fund deviates from the benchmark). The cleanest measure of an active manager's skill.

IRA = 2.5 / 5 = 0.50 IRB = 0.5 / 3 = 0.17

The nuance that separates analysts from amateurs

Sharpe says B; the Information Ratio says A. They answer different questions. Sharpe asks "best reward for total risk?" — B. IR asks "most skill in beating the benchmark?" — A. A fund can be a great standalone holding (high Sharpe) yet a weak active bet (low IR), or vice-versa. Always match the ratio to the decision.

docs.title: 5. Seeing it — the risk-return scatter
docs.text: Each fund is a point (risk on the x-axis, return on the y-axis). Draw a line from the risk-free rate to a fund: its slope is the Sharpe ratio. The steeper the line, the more return that fund earned per unit of risk. Fund B's line is steeper than Fund A's even though A is higher up — the visual proof that B is the better risk-adjusted performer. Illustrative figures.

docs.title: 6. Consistency — rolling returns beat point-to-point
docs.text: A single CAGR or Sharpe over one window can be a fluke of start/end dates. Rolling returns — recomputing over every possible window (e.g. all 3-year periods, rolled daily) — reveal consistency: the average, the range, and how often the fund beat its benchmark. A fund with a slightly lower average but a much tighter, more reliable rolling distribution is often the better real-world holding, because investors can actually stay the course (M6). Prefer rolling-return analysis for any serious comparison.

docs.title: 7. Common mistakes & Do's and Don'ts
docs.text:
Misuses of these ratios

  1. Comparing Sharpe across different periods or asset classes. A debt fund's Sharpe and an equity fund's Sharpe over different windows are not comparable.
  2. Annualisation errors. σ scales with √time, returns compound — don't mix monthly and annual figures.
  3. Too-short a sample. A 1-year Sharpe is noise; use 3–5+ years and rolling windows.
  4. Reading Sharpe alone. It penalises upside volatility too — pair it with Sortino and max drawdown.
  5. Ignoring which risk matters. Standalone holding → Sharpe; sleeve of a portfolio → Treynor; active-skill question → IR.

Do

  • Do annualise consistently and use 3–5+ year rolling windows.
  • Do pick the ratio that matches the decision (total / downside / market / active risk).
  • Do look at max drawdown — the number that predicts whether you'll hold on.

Don't

  • Don't rank funds on raw return.
  • Don't compare risk-adjusted numbers across mismatched periods or categories.

docs.title: 8. Applicable SEBI (Mutual Funds) Regulations, 2026
docs.text:
• Standardised performance & risk disclosure — schemes disclose returns against the TRI benchmark and standard risk metrics, enabling like-for-like risk-adjusted comparison (M7). [verify circular ref]
  • Riskometer & product labelling — the regulatory expression of the risk these ratios quantify. [verify]
  • Benchmarking norms — two-tier/TRI benchmarks underpin the Information Ratio and active-return measurement. [verify]
  • Risk-adjusted ratios themselves are analytical tools, not regulatory mandates; SEBI mandates the disclosures that feed them.

docs.title: 9. Key takeaways
docs.text:
Key takeaways

  • Judge funds on return per unit of risk, never raw return.
  • Sharpe (total risk), Sortino (downside), Treynor (market/beta), IR (active skill) — each answers a different question; match the ratio to the decision.
  • In the worked set, Fund B beat Fund A on Sharpe/Sortino/Treynor despite lower return, yet A led on IR — different questions, different winners.
  • On a risk-return chart, Sharpe is the slope from the risk-free rate.
  • Use max drawdown and rolling returns to judge survivability and consistency — not one lucky window.

docs.title: 10. A word from the field
docs.text:
On what risk really is

"Risk means more things can happen than will happen."

— Howard Marks, The Most Important Thing. Risk-adjusted measures exist precisely because the single outcome you observed (a return) was drawn from a range of things that could have happened — and the width of that range, not just the point you landed on, is what you must price.

docs.title: M10 · Alpha, Beta, Attribution & Factors
docs.text:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Define beta and alpha precisely, estimate them by regression, and use the CAPM line to separate market return from skill.
  • Read R² / correlation and explain why alpha from a low-R² fund is unreliable.
  • Decompose outperformance with Brinson attribution (allocation vs selection vs interaction).
  • Use tracking error and active share to spot closet indexing, and apply the factor lens (size, value, momentum, quality, low-vol) to ask how much "alpha" is really factor exposure.

Builds directly on M9 (it used beta and alpha; here we derive them). Together M9–M10 are the analyst's measurement core, feeding portfolio internals (M11) and construction (M15–M16).

docs.title: 1. Intuition first — how much of the return was the market, and how much was skill?
docs.text:
A fund returned 18% in a year the market rose 16%. Did the manager add value? Not necessarily. If the fund simply takes more market risk (a high beta), 18% might be less than its risk entitled it to. To judge a manager you must first strip out the part of the return that came free from the market, and look at what's left. That residue — the return the manager added beyond what market exposure explains — is alpha. Everything in this module is machinery for isolating it.

flowchart LR
    TR["A fund's return"] --> RF["Risk-free part<br/>(Rf)"]
    TR --> MKT["Market part<br/>β × (Rm − Rf)"]
    TR --> FAC["Factor tilts<br/>size · value · momentum · quality"]
    TR --> AL["True alpha<br/>(skill — what's left)"]
    classDef hi fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20;
    class AL hi;

docs.title: 2. Beta and the CAPM line
docs.text:
Definition — Beta (β)

Beta measures a fund's sensitivity to its benchmark. It is the slope when the fund's excess return (over Rf) is regressed on the market's excess return. β = 1 moves with the market; β = 1.2 amplifies it by 20%; β = 0.8 dampens it.

Formula — CAPM expected return

E(Rp) = Rf + β (Rm - Rf) The return the fund should earn given its market risk alone. Anything above this line is alpha.

docs.title: Worked example 1 — what return did the risk entitle the fund to?
docs.text: Rf = 6.5%, market Rm = 11.5%, fund β = 1.10: E(Rp) = 6.5 + 1.10 (11.5 - 6.5) = 6.5 + 5.5 = 12.0% The fund's market risk "entitled" it to 12%. If it actually delivered 14%, the extra 2% is alpha (next section). If it delivered only 11%, it destroyed value despite a positive return.

docs.title: 3. Alpha — the skill residue
docs.text:
Formula — Jensen's alpha

α = Rp - [Rf + β (Rm - Rf)] Realised return minus the CAPM-expected return. Positive α = return beyond what market risk explains = manager value-add.

For Example 1: α = 14 − 12 = +2.0%. The manager added two percentage points the market did not hand over for free.

docs.title: Seeing beta and alpha together — the regression
docs.text:
When you regress a fund's monthly excess returns on the market's, the slope is beta and the intercept is alpha:

Each dot is one month. The fitted line's slope (≈1.05) is beta; where it crosses the vertical axis (≈ +0.26%/month ≈ +3.2%/year) is alpha. A manager with skill sits the cloud of points above a pure-beta line. Illustrative data.

docs.title: 4. R² — is the alpha even trustworthy?
docs.text:
Definition — R² (and correlation)

R² is the share of a fund's return variance explained by the benchmark (0 to 1). High R² (say 0.90) → the benchmark is the right yardstick and beta/alpha are meaningful. Low R² (say 0.50) → the fund moves to its own drummer, so the regression fit is loose and its alpha is statistically unreliable — a large measured alpha may be noise.

Don't trust alpha without R²

A flashy alpha from a fund with R² = 0.5 is largely meaningless — beta is poorly estimated, so the "skill residue" is mostly measurement error. Always read alpha and R² together. (This is also why a sector fund's alpha vs a broad index is suspect — wrong benchmark, low R².)

docs.title: 5. Attribution — where did the outperformance come from?
docs.text:
Beta/alpha say how much value was added; attribution says from where. The Brinson model splits active return (fund − benchmark) into allocation (over/under-weighting sectors) and selection (picking better stocks within sectors), plus an interaction term.

Formulas — Brinson attribution (per sector, summed)

  • Allocation = (wp - wb) (Rbsector - Rbtotal)
  • Selection = wb (Rpsector - Rbsector)
  • Interaction = (wp - wb) (Rpsector - Rbsector)

docs.title: Worked example 2 — decomposing a manager's edge
docs.text:
A fund vs a two-sector benchmark. Benchmark total return = 0.5×10 + 0.5×14 = 12%.

Sector Bench wt Fund wt Bench return Fund return Financials 50% 60% 10% 12% IT 50% 40% 14% 13%

Fund total = 0.6×12 + 0.4×13 = 12.4%, so active return = +0.4%. Decomposing:

  • Allocation: Fin (0.10)(10−12) = −0.2%; IT (−0.10)(14−12) = −0.2% → −0.4% (over-weighting the lower-returning sector hurt).
  • Selection: Fin 0.5(12−10) = +1.0%; IT 0.5(13−14) = −0.5% → +0.5% (good Financials picks).
  • Interaction: Fin (0.10)(2) + IT (−0.10)(−1) = +0.3%.
  • Check: −0.4 + 0.5 + 0.3 = +0.4% ✓

Reading it: the manager's edge came entirely from stock selection in Financials; their sector allocation actually subtracted value. Two funds with identical +0.4% active return can have opposite skill profiles — attribution reveals which is repeatable.

docs.title: 6. Tracking error, active share, and closet indexing
docs.text:
Definitions

  • Tracking error (TE) — the standard deviation of the fund's active return (fund − benchmark). Low TE = hugs the index; high TE = bold deviations. (TE is the denominator of the Information Ratio, M9.)
  • Active share — the fraction of holdings that differ from the benchmark: 12Σ|wp - wb|. 0% = the index; 100% = nothing in common.

Closet indexing — paying active fees for an index

A fund with low active share (e.g. <50%) but a full active TER is a closet indexer: it barely deviates from the benchmark yet charges for active management. You are paying ~1% (M4) for what an index fund delivers for ~0.2%. High active share doesn't guarantee skill, but low active share guarantees you're overpaying for whatever skill exists.

docs.title: 7. The factor lens — how much "alpha" is really factor exposure?
docs.text:
Decades of research (Fama-French and beyond) show that much of what looks like alpha is actually systematic exposure to factors:

Factor Tilt that earns it Size (SMB) smaller companies Value (HML) cheap (low P/B, P/E) stocks Momentum recent winners Quality high-ROE, low-debt, stable earnings Low volatility less-volatile stocks

The humbling insight

A small-cap value fund that "beats the Nifty" may have zero true alpha — its excess return is the size and value factors, not stock-picking skill. A multi-factor regression (adding size/value/momentum terms to CAPM) strips these out; the intercept that survives is the real alpha. Most of it usually disappears. This is why factor/smart-beta index funds can capture cheaply what active managers charge for — a central theme of M15–M16.

docs.title: 8. Common mistakes & Do's and Don'ts
docs.text:
Analytical traps

  1. Reading alpha without R². Low R² → unreliable alpha.
  2. Crediting alpha that is really beta or factor tilt. Strip market and factors first.
  3. Wrong benchmark. Alpha vs an ill-fitting index (sector fund vs broad market) is meaningless.
  4. Paying for closet indexing. Low active share + active fee = overpaying.
  5. Confusing allocation luck with selection skill — use attribution.

Do

  • Do report alpha with its R², beta and the correct benchmark.
  • Do run attribution to see if skill is in selection (repeatable) or allocation (often luck).
  • Do check active share before paying active fees; consider factor funds for cheap factor exposure.

Don't

  • Don't call factor exposure "alpha".
  • Don't trust a single-period alpha — verify persistence (rolling, M9).

docs.title: 9. Applicable SEBI (Mutual Funds) Regulations, 2026
docs.text:
• Two-tier / TRI benchmarking — the regulatory basis for computing beta, alpha, TE and the Information Ratio against the right yardstick (M7, M9). [verify circular ref]
  • Performance & portfolio disclosure — standardised disclosures that supply the return series and holdings these analytics require. [verify]
  • True-to-label / categorisation — ensures the benchmark fits the fund, keeping R² and alpha meaningful (M3). [verify]
  • These are analytical methods; SEBI mandates the disclosures, not the ratios.

docs.title: 10. Key takeaways
docs.text:
Key takeaways

  • Beta = market sensitivity (regression slope); alpha = return beyond CAPM (intercept) = skill residue.
  • Read alpha with R² — low R² makes alpha unreliable.
  • Attribution splits outperformance into allocation vs selection — only some of it is repeatable skill.
  • Low active share + active fee = closet indexing — you're overpaying.
  • Much "alpha" is factor exposure (size/value/momentum/quality/low-vol); a multi-factor check often dissolves it.

docs.title: 11. A word from the field
docs.text:
On the arithmetic of skill

"Properly measured, the average actively managed dollar must underperform the average passively managed dollar, net of costs."

— William F. Sharpe, "The Arithmetic of Active Management" (1991); Nobel laureate and namesake of the Sharpe ratio. Alpha is a zero-sum game before costs and negative after them — which is why isolating genuine, persistent alpha (not beta or factor tilt dressed up as skill) is so hard, and so valuable.

docs.title: M11 · Portfolio Internals & Debt Analytics
docs.text:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Measure an equity portfolio's internal structure — concentration (top-10, HHI), sector/cap exposure, overlap between funds, and turnover cost.
  • Define YTM, Macaulay and modified duration, and use the price-yield relation to predict a debt fund's NAV move when rates change.
  • Explain convexity, credit spreads, rating migration, and the PRC matrix in depth.
  • Recognise structural risks — liquidity / stress testing, AT1 / perpetual bonds, and side-pocketing.

Builds on M7 (where these numbers appear on a factsheet) and M9–M10 (risk and beta). This module opens the hood: a fund's behaviour is determined by its internals, so reading them is how you anticipate risk before it shows up in returns.

docs.title: 1. Intuition first — the return is in the holdings
docs.text: A fund is not a black box; it is a specific portfolio of securities, and every risk it carries is visible inside that portfolio if you know what to measure. For equity, the questions are: how concentrated, how exposed, how much trading? For debt, two forces dominate everything: interest-rate risk (duration) and credit risk (will issuers pay?). Master those two and you can read any debt fund.

docs.title: 2.1 Concentration — top-10 and the HHI
docs.text:
Beyond the top-10 weight (M7), the Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) captures concentration in one number:

Formula — HHI (concentration)

HHI = Σi wi² (weights as decimals) Higher = more concentrated. An equal-weighted 50-stock fund has HHI ≈ 0.02; a fund with a few dominant bets scores far higher. 1/HHI gives the effective number of stocks.

docs.title: Worked example 1 — two funds, same count, different concentration
docs.text: Both hold 20 stocks. Fund P is equal-weighted (5% each): HHI = 20 × 0.05² = 0.05 → effective stocks = 1/0.05 = 20. Fund Q has 4 stocks at 15% and 16 at 2.5%: HHI = 4(0.15²) + 16(0.025²) = 0.09 + 0.01 = 0.10 → effective stocks = 10. Same 20 holdings, but Q is twice as concentrated — its result will hinge on four names. The count alone lied; HHI told the truth.

docs.title: 2.2 Overlap between funds
docs.text:
Holding three "different" funds that own the same stocks is false diversification (recall the 2026 50%-overlap rule, M3).

Formula — portfolio overlap

Overlap = Σi (wiA, wiB) summed over common holdings. 70% overlap means the two funds are 70% the same portfolio.

docs.title: 2.3 Turnover — the hidden cost of churn
docs.text: Portfolio turnover (M7) is how much the manager traded. High turnover means more brokerage and impact cost (now capped at 6 bps cash / 2 bps derivatives, M4) plus more taxable churn — a drag that must be justified by alpha. High turnover with no outperformance is pure leakage.

docs.title: 3.1 Yield to Maturity (YTM)
docs.text: YTM is the annualised return if every holding is held to maturity and all coupons are reinvested at that yield — a gross indicator of the portfolio's earning power (before TER and defaults). A higher YTM is not free: it usually signals longer duration or weaker credit.

docs.title: 3.2 Duration — interest-rate sensitivity
docs.text:
Formulas — Macaulay & modified duration

Macaulay duration = the cash-flow-weighted average time (years) to receive a bond's payments: DMac = (Σt t · CFt / ((1+y)t)) / Price Modified duration converts it into price sensitivity: Dmod = DMac / (1 + y/n) and (Δ P) / P ≈ - Dmod × Δ y where n = compounding periods/year. A modified duration of 4 means +1% in yields → ~−4% in price.

docs.title: Worked example 2 — what a rate move does to a debt fund's NAV
docs.text: A debt fund reports modified duration 3.5. The RBI surprises with a +0.50% rate move (yields rise): (Δ NAV) / NAV ≈ -3.5 × 0.50% = -1.75% The NAV falls ~1.75% — overnight, with no default involved. A long-duration gilt (duration 8) would fall ~4%, while an overnight fund (duration ~0) barely moves. Duration is the single most important number for predicting a debt fund's volatility.

docs.title: 3.3 Convexity — why duration under-states the truth
docs.text:
Duration is a straight-line estimate, but the real price-yield relationship is curved (convex):

The blue curve is the actual bond price as yields change; the red dashed line is the duration (tangent) estimate. Because the curve lies above the tangent on both sides, prices fall less and rise more than duration alone predicts — that gap is convexity, and it works in the bondholder's favour. Duration is accurate for small moves; for large moves you must add the convexity correction. Illustrative 10-yr 7% bond.

docs.title: 3.4 Credit risk — spread, migration, default
docs.text:
Definitions — credit

  • Credit spread — the extra yield over a government bond of equal maturity, compensating for default risk. Wider spread = market sees more risk.
  • Rating migration — a holding's rating moving (e.g. AA → A, or a downgrade to default). A downgrade widens its spread and cuts its price even before any default.
  • Accrual vs duration strategy — accrual funds earn from coupons/credit (hold to maturity, take credit risk); duration funds bet on rate moves. Reading which a fund does tells you its real risk.

docs.title: 4. The PRC matrix, in depth
docs.text:
The Potential Risk Class matrix caps how much risk a debt scheme may take, on two axes:

Credit Risk I(CRV ≥ 12, safest) Credit Risk II(CRV ≥ 10) Credit Risk III(CRV < 10) Class A — Mac duration ≤ 1 yr A-I A-II A-III Class B — Mac duration ≤ 3 yr B-I B-II B-III Class C — Mac duration > 3 yr (any) C-I C-II C-III

A scheme commits to a cell; A-I is the safest (short duration, highest credit), C-III the riskiest. The matrix lets you compare debt funds' maximum permitted risk at a glance, independent of marketing labels.

docs.title: 5. Structural risks you must know
docs.text:
Three debt-fund landmines

  1. Liquidity risk & stress testing. In a rush of redemptions a fund may be unable to sell illiquid paper without crashing its price. SEBI mandates stress testing and minimum liquid-asset holdings for open-ended debt funds, and liquidity stress-test disclosure for mid/small-cap equity funds (days to liquidate 25%/50% of the portfolio).
  2. AT1 / perpetual bonds. Additional Tier-1 bank bonds have no maturity and can be written down to zero in a crisis (as Indian investors learned in the 2020 Yes Bank episode). SEBI tightened their valuation and capped fund exposure. Treat high AT1 holdings as an equity-like risk hiding in a debt fund.
  3. Side-pocketing (segregated portfolios). When a holding defaults, the AMC may carve it into a separate "side pocket" so the main NAV reflects only good assets; you keep a claim on any future recovery. It protects fresh investors from a stale bad asset — but its presence signals the fund took credit risk that soured.

docs.title: 6. Common mistakes & Do's and Don'ts
docs.text:
Internals traps

  1. Judging diversification by stock count, not HHI/overlap. Twenty stocks can be as concentrated as ten.
  2. Chasing YTM. A high YTM usually means longer duration or weaker credit, not free return.
  3. Ignoring duration before a rate move. Duration is the volatility forecast.
  4. Assuming "debt = safe". AT1 write-downs and defaults can hit hard.
  5. Overlooking turnover cost when it isn't earning alpha.

Do

  • Do read HHI/overlap for true diversification, and duration + PRC + credit profile for debt risk.
  • Do match a debt fund's duration to your horizon (short duration for short horizons).
  • Do check AT1 exposure, liquidity/stress disclosure, and any side pocket.

Don't

  • Don't equate a high YTM with a better fund.
  • Don't hold multiple high-overlap funds and call it diversified.

docs.title: 7. Applicable SEBI (Mutual Funds) Regulations, 2026
docs.text:
• PRC matrix — mandatory potential-risk-class disclosure for debt schemes. [verify section no.]
  • Liquidity & stress testing — minimum liquid assets for open-ended debt; stress-test disclosure for debt and mid/small-cap equity schemes. [verify]
  • Valuation norms — fair valuation including the tightened AT1/perpetual-bond valuation rules. [verify]
  • Segregated portfolios (side-pocketing) — framework for carving out defaulted/downgraded assets. [verify]
  • Portfolio disclosure — periodic (fortnightly for debt) holdings disclosure that makes these analytics possible. [verify]

(The full valuation, liquidity and disclosure architecture is consolidated in M18.)

docs.title: 8. Key takeaways
docs.text:
Key takeaways

  • A fund's risk lives in its internals — read them before the returns surprise you.
  • Equity: use HHI and overlap (not stock count) for real concentration/diversification; watch turnover cost.
  • Debt is governed by duration (rate risk: ΔP ≈ −ModDur × Δy) and credit (spread, migration, default); convexity improves on duration for big moves.
  • The PRC matrix caps a debt fund's permitted duration × credit risk — compare funds by their cell.
  • Know the landmines: liquidity/stress, AT1 write-downs, side-pocketing.

docs.title: 9. A word from the field
docs.text:
On reaching for yield

"More money has been lost reaching for yield than at the point of a gun."

— Raymond DeVoe Jr., market strategist. A debt fund's higher YTM is almost always paid for in duration or credit risk. Reading the internals — duration, PRC, credit profile, AT1 and liquidity — is how you tell a fair yield from a dangerous one.

docs.title: M12 · Valuation-Aware Entry
docs.text:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Read the core market-valuation signals — P/E, P/B, dividend yield, earnings yield vs bond yield (the yield gap), and market-cap-to-GDP.
  • Explain why high starting valuations lower expected forward returns — and why this sets the odds, not the timing.
  • Convert valuation into a deployment-pace decision (lump vs tranched STP) rather than a market-timing bet.
  • Decompose expected return into earnings growth + dividend yield ± valuation change.

This is the quantitative upgrade of M6's "when to enter". It uses the risk vocabulary of M9–M11 and feeds rebalancing (M13) and construction (M15).

Not a market call

Nothing here predicts the market or recommends buying/selling at a moment. Valuation shifts probabilities over years; it is useless as a short-term timer. Figures are illustrative.

docs.title: 1. Intuition first — valuation sets the odds, not the date
docs.text:
Markets are not a coin flip independent of price. What you pay shapes what you earn — buy when the market is expensive and your expected multi-year return is lower; buy when it's cheap and the odds tilt your way. But — and this is the whole discipline — valuation tells you nothing about when. An expensive market can get more expensive for years. So a professional doesn't time entry on valuation; they adjust the pace of deployment and the expectations they set.

Schematic (not a forecast): each dot is a hypothetical starting valuation and the return that followed. The cloud slopes down — higher starting P/E tends to precede lower multi-year returns — but the scatter is wide, which is exactly why valuation governs odds and pace, not a precise entry date.

docs.title: 2. The core valuation signals
docs.text:
Definitions — the market gauges

  • P/E (price-to-earnings) — price per ₹1 of earnings. High = expensive (or high growth priced in).
  • Earnings yield = 1 / P/E — the earnings you "buy" per ₹100 invested; directly comparable to a bond yield.
  • P/B (price-to-book) — price per ₹1 of net assets; useful where earnings are cyclical (banks).
  • Dividend yield — income per ₹100; low yield often accompanies rich valuations.
  • Market-cap-to-GDP ("Buffett indicator") — total market value vs the economy's size; a long-run rich/cheap gauge.

docs.title: 2.1 The yield gap — equities vs bonds
docs.text:
The most decision-useful signal compares the earnings yield of equities with the government-bond yield:

Formula — the yield gap (equity risk premium proxy)

Yield gap = 1 / P/E - R10y G-sec A positive, wide gap means equities are relatively cheap vs bonds; a thin or negative gap means equities are richly priced relative to the safe alternative.

docs.title: Worked example 1 — reading the yield gap
docs.text: The Nifty trades at a trailing P/E of 22 → earnings yield = 1/22 = 4.55%. The 10-year G-sec yields 7.0%. Yield gap = 4.55% - 7.0% = -2.45% Equities are yielding less than the risk-free bond — a rich reading. The response is not "sell" or "don't invest"; it is "deploy fresh lump sums more slowly, set lower return expectations, and keep rebalancing." Contrast: at a P/E of 15, earnings yield = 6.67%, gap = −0.33% — far less stretched, so deploy faster.

docs.title: 3. From valuation to deployment pace
docs.text:
The practical output of valuation analysis is how fast to put a lump sum to work — a direct upgrade of the STP idea from M6.

flowchart TD
    V{"Where is the market<br/>vs its own history?"}
    V -- "Cheap band<br/>(low P/E, wide yield gap)" --> C["Deploy faster<br/>(lump or short 3–6m STP)"]
    V -- "Fair band" --> F["Normal SIP / 6–9m STP"]
    V -- "Expensive band<br/>(high P/E, thin/neg gap)" --> E["Stagger slowly<br/>(12–24m STP, more in liquid)"]
    classDef c fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#1b5e20;
    classDef e fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,color:#b71c1c;
    class C c;
    class E e;

docs.title: Worked example 2 — staggering ₹12,00,000 by valuation
docs.text:
You have ₹12,00,000 to deploy with the market in an expensive band (Example 1). Instead of a lump sum, you park it in a liquid fund and run a valuation-aware STP over 18 months (₹66,667/month), with a rule to accelerate transfers if the market falls into the fair/cheap band. Outcomes:

  • If the market corrects, you deploy the rest faster and cheaper — valuation rewarded your patience.
  • If it keeps rising, you gave up some upside on the parked balance (which still earned ~6–7% in liquid) — the price of insurance against entering at a peak.

The point is not to predict which happens, but to size the regret: when valuations are stretched, the cost of being wrong about a lump sum is highest, so you stagger.

docs.title: 4. Decomposing expected return (setting honest expectations)
docs.text:
Formula — return decomposition

Expected return ≈ earnings growth + dividend yield ± valuation change

docs.title: Worked example 3 — where future return must come from
docs.text: At a P/E of 22, suppose earnings grow 12%/yr and dividend yield is 1.3%. If valuations hold, expected return ≈ 13.3%. But if the P/E mean-reverts to 18 over five years, that's a valuation drag of roughly −4%/yr of multiple compression, cutting expected return to ~9%. This is why entering at high valuations lowers expected returns mechanically — you are relying on earnings to outrun a shrinking multiple. Setting this expectation up front is what keeps an investor from panic when a richly-bought market merely normalises.

docs.title: 5. How Balanced Advantage Funds automate this
docs.text: Dynamic Asset Allocation / Balanced Advantage Funds (BAFs) (M3) operationalise valuation-aware entry inside a single fund: a model raises equity when valuations (P/E, P/B, yield gap) are low and trims it when they're high. For an investor who won't run their own STP discipline, a BAF outsources the pace decision — at the cost of trusting the AMC's model and a higher TER than a plain index fund.

docs.title: 6. Common mistakes & Do's and Don'ts
docs.text:
Valuation traps

  1. Using valuation as a short-term timer. Expensive markets can stay expensive for years; cheap can get cheaper.
  2. Stopping SIPs because "the market is high". SIPs are horizon tools (M6); valuation adjusts lump-sum pace, not your monthly discipline.
  3. Single-metric tunnel vision. A high P/E can be justified by a rate cut or earnings surge; read the yield gap and growth, not P/E alone.
  4. "This time is different" at the top, or refusing to invest at all at the bottom.
  5. Comparing P/E across markets/sectors without adjusting for growth and composition.

Do

  • Do let valuation set deployment pace and expectations, not entry dates.
  • Do read the yield gap alongside P/E, and keep SIPs running regardless.
  • Do stagger lump sums more when valuations are stretched; consider a BAF to automate it.

Don't

  • Don't try to call tops and bottoms.
  • Don't abandon a long-horizon plan over a valuation reading.

docs.title: 7. Applicable SEBI (Mutual Funds) Regulations, 2026
docs.text:
• Dynamic Asset Allocation / BAF category — the regulated vehicle that implements valuation-based equity shifting (M3). [verify section no.]
  • Riskometer & disclosures — risk labelling that should accompany any valuation-driven product. [verify]
  • Performance-claim / advertising norms — restrictions on implying market-timing ability or guaranteed returns. [verify]
  • Valuation analysis itself is an analytical discipline, not a regulation; SEBI governs the products and disclosures around it.

docs.title: 8. Key takeaways
docs.text:
Key takeaways

  • What you pay shapes what you earn — high starting valuations lower expected multi-year returns.
  • Valuation sets the odds and the pace, never the date — it is a poor short-term timer.
  • Read the yield gap (earnings yield − bond yield) with P/E, P/B, dividend yield and market-cap/GDP.
  • Convert valuation into deployment pace: deploy faster when cheap, stagger via STP when expensive; keep SIPs running.
  • Decompose expected return into earnings growth + dividend ± valuation change to set honest expectations; BAFs automate the pace.

docs.title: 9. A word from the field
docs.text:
On price versus value

"Price is what you pay; value is what you get."

— Warren Buffett (echoing his teacher Benjamin Graham). Valuation-aware entry is simply taking this seriously: the price level of the market when you deploy is one of the few things you can observe in advance, so let it govern your pace and expectations — without pretending it tells you the day.

docs.title: M13 · Monitoring & Rebalancing
docs.text:
Learning objectives

By the end of this module you will be able to:

  • Separate monitoring (a periodic review) from tinkering, and know what to watch and what to ignore.
  • Apply the three rebalancing methods — calendar, threshold (tolerance-band), and hybrid — and compute the trade a drift requires.
  • Rebalance tax- and cost-efficiently using new cash flows, STP/SWP and the ₹1.25 lakh exemption.
  • Run a goal glide path, and decide when monitoring should trigger a switch versus patience.

This is the quantitative version of M6's "how long to stay / when to shift". It uses risk metrics (M9–M11), valuation (M12), and tax (M8), and sets up the exit calculus of M14.

docs.title: 1. Intuition first — rebalancing controls risk, it doesn't chase return
docs.text: Left alone, a portfolio drifts. A long equity rally quietly turns a balanced 60/40 portfolio into a racy 70/30 one — so just when the market is most expensive (M12), your risk is highest. Rebalancing sells what has grown and buys what has lagged, dragging the portfolio back to its chosen risk. It is systematic sell-high, buy-low, executed by a rule rather than a feeling. Crucially, its main job is risk control, not return maximisation: in a one-way bull market, rebalancing slightly lowers return (you trimmed the winner) — but it stops you carrying risk you never signed up for into the next crash.

docs.title: 2. Monitoring — what to watch, what to ignore
docs.text:
Monitor (quarterly / semi-annually)

  • Asset-allocation drift — has equity/debt/gold moved beyond your bands?
  • Mandate drift / true-to-label — is the fund still doing what its category says (M3, M7)?
  • Manager or strategy change — a key-person change weakens the track record's relevance.
  • Persistent under-performance vs category (not one quarter) and rising costs.
  • Risk metrics — has σ, drawdown, duration or credit quality crept up (M9–M11)?

Ignore (it's noise)

  • Daily NAV and one-week/one-month moves.
  • A single weak quarter from an otherwise sound fund.
  • Headlines and tips. Over-monitoring causes over-trading.

The cadence matters: review on a schedule, act only on a rule. Looking daily tempts you to act; reviewing quarterly lets signal separate from noise.

docs.title: 3. The three rebalancing methods
docs.text:
Definitions — rebalancing methods

  • Calendar — rebalance on a fixed date (e.g. once a year), regardless of drift. Simple, disciplined, but blind to big mid-period moves.
  • Threshold (tolerance band) — rebalance only when an asset drifts beyond a band (e.g. target 60% ± 5 → act if equity < 55% or > 65%). Responsive to markets; ignores small wiggles.
  • Hybrid (calendar + threshold) — check on a schedule, but trade only if a band is breached. The professional default: disciplined and efficient.

Target equity 60% with a ±5% band (the green zone). The portfolio's equity weight drifts up as markets rise; each time it breaches 65%, a rebalance sells equity back to 60%. Between breaches you do nothing — avoiding needless cost. Illustrative.

docs.title: 4. The drift math
docs.text:
Formulas — drift and the rebalancing trade

  • Current weight w = (asset value) / (total portfolio)
  • Drift = current weight − target weight; act if |drift| > band.
  • Trade to restore target = (current weight − target weight) × total portfolio.

docs.title: Worked example 1 — a threshold rebalance
docs.text:
Target 60/40 on a portfolio that has grown to ₹13,20,000, now ₹8,71,200 equity / ₹4,48,800 debt → equity weight = 8,71,200 ÷ 13,20,000 = 66.0%. That breaches the 65% upper band, so rebalance to 60%:

Target equity = 0.60 × 13,20,000 = ₹7,92,000 Sell equity = 8,71,200 - 7,92,000 = ₹79,200 → into debt

You have just sold equity high and bought debt low, restoring 60/40 — with no forecast, only a rule.

docs.title: 5. Rebalancing without bleeding cost and tax
docs.text:
A naïve rebalance sells, triggering exit load + capital-gains tax (M8) and the friction warned of in M6. Smarter methods cut that to near zero:

The tax-and-cost-aware toolkit
